judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all four expectations with comparable depth and concrete fixes; Report 2 adds a few extra observations (grep-confirmed absence of imports, per-applicant vs vendor-level quota gap) but neither is clearly stronger on the graded expectations.

report_1.evidence: BadRequestError — caught, falls over. Wrong: a deterministic rejection of the payload will fail identically at the secondary vendor... ContentPolicyError — caught, falls over. Wrong, and the most serious case... AuthError — caught, falls over. Wrong: this signals broken credentials/configuration on our side... Concrete fix: add an explicit, tested denylist function, e.g. def is_non_retryable(exc): return isinstance(exc, (BadRequestError, ContentPolicyError, AuthError))... and in call_with_fallback re-raise (never fall over) when it returns True; fall over only on (RateLimitError, RequestTimeoutError, ServerError) plus connection errors, not on bare Exception.

report_2.evidence: BadRequestError — inappropriate. A malformed payload is deterministic... ContentPolicyError — inappropriate and a compliance concern... AuthError — inappropriate. An auth failure means credentials are broken... Concrete fix: in router.py, import the exception classes and catch only the transient ones — except (RateLimitError, RequestTimeoutError, ServerError) → fall back; re-raise BadRequestError, ContentPolicyError, and AuthError (with alerting on AuthError); let everything else propagate

report_1.evidence: Path A... Any environment where STAGE is unset, empty, or spelled anything other than exactly "production"... gets no rate limiting at all. Unsound... Path D (lines 17–18, except RedisError): returns True. If the redis backend errors, the limiter fails open and admits the call. Unsound: a redis outage silently disables the only spend/abuse gate in the pipeline. A safety gate should fail closed on backend failure. Concrete fix: ...(c) change the except RedisError branch to return False

report_2.evidence: This is fail-open on configuration: the safety mechanism silently vanishes if the environment is mis-declared... Fail-open on infrastructure failure: if redis is down, every request is allowed, and nothing is logged... for a pipeline sending sensitive data to paid vendor APIs I would fail closed (or fall back to an in-process limiter).

report_1.evidence: input_text (the entire sensitive intake document, verbatim), and metadata (the whole dict, unfiltered — whatever any future caller puts in it gets logged). Concrete fix: ...replace input_text with a content hash plus length... never the text itself; and filter metadata through an explicit allowlist of known-safe keys... dropping everything else, rather than trying to enumerate unsafe keys.

report_2.evidence: record.input_text — the entire document text, which per the task and README contains sensitive personal information; and record.metadata, unfiltered. Concrete fix: ...drop input_text entirely — if a content reference is needed, log a length and/or a content hash — and either allowlist specific metadata keys or drop it.

report_1.evidence: when it is unset or empty the function silently degrades to an unsalted sha256(applicant_id). ... Concrete fix: fail closed — salt = os.environ["PII_SALT"] plus an explicit if not salt: raise RuntimeError("PII_SALT must be set and non-empty")

report_2.evidence: if PII_SALT is unset (or set to empty), the default "" is used and the function silently degrades to a plain unsalted SHA-256 of the applicant id. No error, no warning... Concrete fix: raise at startup (or on first call) if PII_SALT is missing or empty — e.g., salt = os.environ["PII_SALT"] plus an explicit emptiness check
